title: Install dependencies and verify GPU
description: Set up the Python environment with all required packages for training, evaluation, and visualization. Verify CUDA availability and GPU memory.

steps.step1: Install core packages: torch, torchvision, numpy, scipy, scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn, pandas, tqdm
steps.step2: Verify CUDA is available and confirm GPU type (RTX A6000, 48GB). Run: python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available()); print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)); print(torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_mem)"
steps.step3: Create directory structure: results/{cifar10,cifar100,tinyimagenet}/{ce,label_smoothing,mixup,ccr_adaptive,ccr_fixed,ccr_spectral}/{seed_0,seed_1,seed_2}/ for checkpoints and logs
steps.step4: Create a shared config.py defining all hyperparameters, paths, and constants used across experiments

title: Download and prepare CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and TinyImageNet
description: Download all three datasets, apply standard preprocessing, and verify data loading works correctly. Also download OOD evaluation datasets (SVHN).

steps.step1: Download CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 via torchvision.datasets (automatic download). Apply standard normalization: CIFAR-10/100 mean=[0.4914, 0.4822, 0.4465], std=[0.2023, 0.1994, 0.2010]
steps.step2: Download TinyImageNet from the official source (http://cs231n.stanford.edu/tiny-imagenet-200.zip). Parse the val annotations to create proper val loader. Apply normalization: mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]
steps.step3: Define training augmentation: RandomCrop(32, padding=4) + RandomHorizontalFlip for CIFAR; RandomCrop(64, padding=8) + RandomHorizontalFlip for TinyImageNet. Test augmentation: only normalization
steps.step4: Download SVHN test set via torchvision for OOD evaluation (to be used as OOD data for CIFAR models)
steps.step5: Verify all dataloaders: print dataset sizes, sample shapes, number of classes. CIFAR-10: 50K/10K, 10 classes. CIFAR-100: 50K/10K, 100 classes. TinyImageNet: 100K/10K, 200 classes
steps.step6: Create a data_utils.py module that returns train/test loaders for any dataset given batch_size=256 and num_workers=4

title: Implement the CCR training framework
description: Implement the core training infrastructure including the Calibrated Collapse Regularizer (CCR) and all its variants. This single codebase supports all methods (CE, label smoothing, Mixup, CCR variants) via configuration flags.

steps.step1: Implement a ResNet-18 model (from torchvision.models.resnet18) modified to expose the penultimate layer features. Add a hook or modify forward() to return both logits and the 512-dim features before the final FC layer. Do the same for ResNet-50 (2048-dim features).
steps.step2: Implement the CCR module with three variants in ccr.py: (a) CCR-fixed: L_CCR = mean_c max(0, tau - S_c) where S_c = mean_i ||z_i - mu_c||^2 for class c. Use tau=1.0 as default. (b) CCR-adaptive: tau = gamma * min_{c!=c'} ||mu_c - mu_c'||^2, with gamma=0.1 as default. (c) CCR-S (spectral): L_CCR_S = -mean_c EffRank(Sigma_c) where EffRank = exp(-sum p_k log p_k), p_k = lambda_k / sum lambda_j. Maintain running class prototypes with EMA momentum alpha=0.999.
steps.step3: Implement the unified training loop in train.py that accepts config flags: --method {ce, label_smoothing, mixup, ccr_fixed, ccr_adaptive, ccr_spectral} --dataset {cifar10, cifar100, tinyimagenet} --arch {resnet18, resnet50} --seed INT --lambda_ccr FLOAT --gamma FLOAT --tau FLOAT --epochs INT --lr FLOAT --batch_size INT --output_dir PATH
steps.step4: Training details: SGD with momentum=0.9, weight_decay=5e-4. Cosine annealing LR from 0.1 to 0. Label smoothing: epsilon=0.1. Mixup: alpha=0.2 applied at input level. CCR: lambda_ccr=0.1 (default), applied to penultimate features.
steps.step5: Save checkpoints every 50 epochs (for NC tracking) and the final model. Each checkpoint saves: model state_dict, optimizer state_dict, epoch, running class prototypes. Also save a training log (JSON lines) with per-epoch: train_loss, train_acc, test_acc, test_loss, and CCR-specific metrics (mean within-class spread, min inter-class distance, CCR loss value).
steps.step6: Implement NC metric computation in nc_metrics.py. After training, compute on the full training set in eval mode: NC1 = tr(Sigma_W @ Sigma_B_inv) / K, NC2 = std of pairwise cosine similarities of centered class means, NC3 = ||W_hat - M_hat||_F (normalized classifier vs normalized class means), NC4 = NCC accuracy vs network accuracy. Save all NC metrics to results JSON.
steps.step7: Implement calibration evaluation in calibration.py. Compute on test set: ECE (15 equal-width bins), MCE, AdaECE (15 equal-mass bins), NLL, Brier score. Also compute per-bin reliability data for reliability diagrams. Save all metrics to results JSON.
steps.step8: Implement temperature scaling in temperature_scaling.py. After training, learn optimal temperature T on a held-out validation set (use 10% of training data as val, split with fixed seed). Apply T to logits and recompute all calibration metrics. Save T and post-TS metrics.

title: Train baselines on all datasets (3 seeds)
description: Train Standard CE, Label Smoothing, and Mixup baselines on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 (3 seeds each) and TinyImageNet (1 seed) using ResNet-18. These provide the comparison points for our method. Estimated time: ~4.5 hours.

steps.step1: Train Standard CE baseline: ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10 with seeds [0, 1, 2], 200 epochs each. Expected: ~12 min/run × 3 = 36 min. Command template: python train.py --method ce --dataset cifar10 --arch resnet18 --seed {0,1,2} --epochs 200 --batch_size 256 --lr 0.1 --output_dir results/cifar10/ce/seed_{s}
steps.step2: Train Standard CE baseline: ResNet-18 on CIFAR-100 with seeds [0, 1, 2], 200 epochs. ~12 min/run × 3 = 36 min.
steps.step3: Train Label Smoothing baseline: ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with seeds [0, 1, 2], 200 epochs. epsilon=0.1. ~12 min/run × 6 = 72 min.
steps.step4: Train Mixup baseline: ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with seeds [0, 1, 2], 200 epochs. alpha=0.2. ~12 min/run × 6 = 72 min.
steps.step5: Train all three baselines on TinyImageNet with seed 0 only, 100 epochs. ~20 min/run × 3 = 60 min.
steps.step6: After all baseline training, run evaluate.py on each checkpoint to compute: test accuracy, top-5 accuracy (CIFAR-100/TinyImageNet), all calibration metrics (ECE, MCE, AdaECE, NLL, Brier), all NC metrics (NC1-NC4), and temperature scaling. Save results to results/{dataset}/{method}/seed_{s}/metrics.json
steps.step7: Verify baseline results are reasonable: CIFAR-10 CE accuracy ~93-95%, CIFAR-100 CE accuracy ~75-78%, TinyImageNet CE accuracy ~55-60%. If results are significantly off, debug before proceeding.
steps.step8: Total estimated time for baselines: CIFAR (36+36+72+72) = 216 min + TinyImageNet 60 min + evaluation 20 min ≈ 5 hrs. Run sequentially on single GPU.

title: Train CCR-adaptive on all datasets (3 seeds)
description: Train our primary method (CCR with adaptive threshold) on all three datasets. This is the main comparison against baselines. Estimated time: ~1.5 hours.

steps.step1: Train CCR-adaptive: ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10 with seeds [0, 1, 2], 200 epochs. lambda_ccr=0.1, gamma=0.1, momentum alpha=0.999. ~12 min/run × 3 = 36 min. Command: python train.py --method ccr_adaptive --dataset cifar10 --arch resnet18 --seed {0,1,2} --lambda_ccr 0.1 --gamma 0.1 --epochs 200 --output_dir results/cifar10/ccr_adaptive/seed_{s}
steps.step2: Train CCR-adaptive: ResNet-18 on CIFAR-100 with seeds [0, 1, 2], 200 epochs. Same hyperparameters. ~12 min/run × 3 = 36 min.
steps.step3: Train CCR-adaptive: ResNet-18 on TinyImageNet with seed 0 only, 100 epochs. ~20 min.
steps.step4: Run full evaluation (accuracy, calibration, NC metrics, temperature scaling) on all CCR-adaptive checkpoints.
steps.step5: Quick sanity check: CCR-adaptive accuracy should be within 1-2% of CE baseline. If accuracy drops >3%, the lambda_ccr is too high — reduce to 0.05 and retrain on one seed before proceeding.
steps.step6: Total estimated time: 36+36+20 = 92 min + evaluation 10 min ≈ 1.7 hrs

title: Ablation studies: CCR variants and hyperparameter sensitivity
description: Ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each novel component. Run on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 (single seed) to save compute. Estimated time: ~1 hour.

steps.step1: CCR-fixed vs CCR-adaptive ablation: Train CCR-fixed (tau=1.0) on CIFAR-100 with seed 0, 200 epochs. Compare against CCR-adaptive (already trained). This tests whether the adaptive threshold is better than a fixed one. ~12 min.
steps.step2: CCR-S (spectral) ablation: Train CCR-S on CIFAR-100 with seed 0, 200 epochs. lambda_ccr=0.1. Compare against CCR-adaptive. This tests whether controlling effective dimensionality helps beyond controlling total variance. ~12 min.
steps.step3: Lambda sensitivity sweep: Train CCR-adaptive on CIFAR-100 with seed 0 at lambda_ccr values [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0]. The lambda=0.1 run is already done. Need 4 additional runs × 12 min = 48 min. This maps the accuracy-ECE Pareto frontier.
steps.step4: Gamma sensitivity: Train CCR-adaptive on CIFAR-100 with seed 0 at gamma values [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5]. The gamma=0.1 run is already done. Need 4 additional runs × 12 min = 48 min. However, to save time, only run gamma=[0.05, 0.25] (2 extra runs, 24 min) in addition to the lambda sweep.
steps.step5: Post-hoc combination: Apply temperature scaling to all CCR-adaptive models (already done in main eval). Compare CCR+TS vs CE+TS to show that better representations help post-hoc calibration.
steps.step6: Evaluate all ablation models with full metrics suite (accuracy, calibration, NC metrics). Save to respective results directories.
steps.step7: Total estimated time: 12+12+48+24 = 96 min + evaluation 10 min ≈ 1.8 hrs

title: Neural collapse tracking during training
description: Analyze how NC metrics evolve during training for CE vs CCR-adaptive. Uses checkpoints saved every 50 epochs (no additional training needed).

steps.step1: For CIFAR-100 seed 0, load checkpoints at epochs [50, 100, 150, 200] for both CE and CCR-adaptive models.
steps.step2: At each checkpoint, compute full NC metrics (NC1, NC2, NC3, NC4) on the training set. Also compute within-class spread per class and inter-class distances.
steps.step3: Verify that CCR-adaptive shows higher NC1 (less collapsed within-class variability) while maintaining similar NC2 (ETF structure) and NC3 (self-duality) compared to CE.
steps.step4: Compute calibration metrics at each checkpoint epoch on the test set to track how calibration evolves with NC degree during training.
steps.step5: Save all per-epoch metrics to results/cifar100/nc_tracking.json for visualization.

title: Statistical evaluation and success criteria verification
description: Aggregate all results, compute statistics across seeds, and verify whether the pre-defined success criteria are met.

steps.step1: Create results_table.py that aggregates all metrics.json files and produces: (a) Main results table: mean ± std across 3 seeds for each method × dataset combination. Columns: Accuracy, Top-5 Acc, ECE, MCE, AdaECE, NLL. (b) NC metrics table: NC1, NC2, NC3, NC4 for each method.
steps.step2: Check PRIMARY success criteria: (a) CCR reduces ECE by ≥20% relative to CE on at least 2/3 datasets. Compute: (ECE_ce - ECE_ccr) / ECE_ce × 100 for each dataset. (b) NC1 metric is measurably higher under CCR while NC2 within 10% of CE. Compute relative differences.
steps.step3: Check SECONDARY success criteria: (a) CCR Pareto-dominates label smoothing and Mixup on accuracy-ECE. For the lambda sweep, plot the frontier and check if any CCR point achieves both higher accuracy and lower ECE. (b) CCR+TS outperforms CE+TS. (c) Rank correlation between NC1 and ECE across the lambda sweep (compute Spearman's rho, expect |rho| > 0.5).
steps.step4: Check REFUTATION conditions: (a) Is Spearman's rho between NC1 and ECE < 0.3? (b) Is accuracy drop > 3%? (c) Does CE+TS match or beat CCR? If any refutation condition is met, note it honestly.
steps.step5: Compute statistical significance: paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank test between CE and CCR-adaptive across seeds, for ECE and accuracy. Report p-values.
steps.step6: Save comprehensive results summary to results/summary.json with all tables, statistics, and success criteria verdicts.

title: Generate all paper figures and tables
description: Create publication-quality figures for the paper. All figures saved as both PDF and PNG.

steps.step1: Figure 1 (Conceptual): Create a 2D illustration showing the key idea: (a) full neural collapse — all class features collapsed to points, all predictions maximally confident; (b) partial collapse with CCR — features maintain spread proportional to boundary proximity, enabling calibrated confidence. Use synthetic 2D Gaussian clusters for clarity.
steps.step2: Figure 2 (Main results bar chart): Grouped bar chart showing ECE (primary) and accuracy (secondary axis) for CE, Label Smoothing, Mixup, CCR-adaptive across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet. Include error bars (std across seeds). Style: seaborn, publication-quality, no gridlines clutter.
steps.step3: Figure 3 (Reliability diagrams): 3×4 grid of reliability diagrams (rows=datasets, columns=methods). Each panel shows expected accuracy vs confidence with 15 bins, identity line, and ECE annotation. Highlight that CCR diagrams are closer to the identity line.
steps.step4: Figure 4 (NC metrics during training): 2×2 subplot showing NC1, NC2, NC3, NC4 vs epoch for CE and CCR-adaptive on CIFAR-100. Show that CCR selectively controls NC1 while NC2/NC3/NC4 are preserved.
steps.step5: Figure 5 (Pareto frontier): Scatter plot of accuracy vs ECE for the lambda sweep (CCR-adaptive). Overlay label smoothing epsilon sweep and Mixup alpha sweep if available, otherwise overlay the 3-seed mean points for each baseline. Show that CCR achieves a superior frontier.
steps.step6: Figure 6 (Ablation heatmap or bar chart): Compare CCR-fixed, CCR-adaptive, CCR-S on CIFAR-100. Show ECE and accuracy for each variant.
steps.step7: Figure 7 (CCR+TS combination): Bar chart showing ECE for CE, CE+TS, CCR, CCR+TS across datasets. Demonstrate that CCR+TS is the best combination.
steps.step8: Table 1 (Main results): LaTeX-formatted table with accuracy, ECE, MCE, NLL for all methods × datasets. Bold best, underline second best. Include ± std from 3 seeds.
steps.step9: Table 2 (NC metrics): LaTeX-formatted table showing NC1, NC2, NC3, NC4 for each method on CIFAR-100.
steps.step10: Table 3 (Ablation): LaTeX-formatted table comparing CCR variants and sensitivity to lambda/gamma.
steps.step11: Save all figures to figures/ directory as PDF and PNG (dpi=300). Save LaTeX tables to tables/ directory.

title: Runtime budget verification
description: Verify the total experiment plan fits within the 8-hour budget on 1 GPU.

steps.step1: Estimated sequential runtime breakdown: (1) Env+Data: 15 min. (2) Baselines [CE×3seeds×2datasets + LS×3seeds×2datasets + Mixup×3seeds×2datasets + 3methods×1seed×TinyImageNet]: 18 CIFAR runs × 12 min + 3 TinyImageNet runs × 20 min = 216 + 60 = 276 min ≈ 4.6 hrs. (3) CCR-adaptive [3seeds×2datasets + 1seed×TinyImageNet]: 6 CIFAR runs × 12 min + 1 TinyImageNet × 20 min = 92 min ≈ 1.5 hrs. (4) Ablations [CCR-fixed + CCR-S + 4 lambda + 2 gamma = 8 runs on CIFAR-100]: 8 × 12 = 96 min ≈ 1.6 hrs. (5) Analysis+Vis: 30 min. TOTAL: 15 + 276 + 92 + 96 + 30 = 509 min ≈ 8.5 hrs.
steps.step2: This is slightly over budget. Mitigation strategies: (a) Reduce CIFAR epochs from 200 to 150 (saves ~25% → ~6.4 hrs for training). (b) Or reduce ablation lambda sweep from 4 to 2 extra values (saves ~24 min). (c) Or run TinyImageNet baselines with seed 0 only (already planned). Apply strategy (a) if initial runs suggest 200 epochs is slow, otherwise apply (b).
steps.step3: Fallback priority if running out of time: (1) MUST complete: CE+CCR-adaptive on CIFAR-10/100 with 3 seeds + full evaluation. (2) HIGH: Label smoothing + Mixup baselines with 3 seeds. (3) MEDIUM: TinyImageNet experiments. (4) MEDIUM: Ablation studies. (5) LOW: Extra lambda/gamma sweep points. (6) LOW: ResNet-50 experiments.
steps.step4: Seeds to use: [42, 123, 456] for reproducibility across all experiments.
